title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: modern web applications built frameworks like Shiny Plumber, running long-running resource-intensive computations directly main request handler blocks user interface thread. leads high latency poor user experience. robust production architecture separates concerns: Web Server (Shiny/Plumber): Quickly accepts user requests, writes jobs persistent queue (like liteq), queries database asynchronously display results. Worker Daemon: background R process runs indefinitely, consumes tasks queue, processes parallel controlled concurrency via siphon, saves results back database. vignette demonstrates implement background daemon pattern using siphon liteq. ’ll build: background daemon consumes persistent queue, processes tasks parallel siphon, persists results, web tier remains responsive. use liteq mirai examples; can swap queues backends. builds core concepts - pump(), pump_source(), pump_drain() - introduced Pull-Based Staged Pipelines siphon (vignette("siphon", package = "siphon")). examples use liteq, mirai, jsonlite packages; install run code .

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: liteq package provides serverless, database-backed queue using SQLite. concurrent-safe, supports lock-free message consumption, ensures messages either permanently acknowledged (ack) returned queue failure (nack), providing transaction-like reliability.

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: web front end enqueues jobs; siphon daemon consumes , processes parallel, persists results.

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: First, turn queue pipeline source commit/abort semantics. source consumes message liteq, decodes JSON payload, emits payload pipeline. original liteq message stays main R process siphon can acknowledge reject item leaves pipeline. pump_managed_source() convenience wrapper sources need per-item control. stores source-owned item internally, emits serializable data, calls commit_fn(), abort_fn(), release_fn() right time.
code: library(siphon)  # Count READY messages in the queue. See "A note on liteq and parallel # backends" below for why this guard is required before try_consume(). n_ready <- function(queue) {     msgs <- liteq::list_messages(queue)     if (nrow(msgs) == 0L) 0L else sum(msgs$status == "READY") }  make_liteq_source <- function(queue, run_forever = TRUE) {     pump_managed_source(         pull_fn = function() {             if (n_ready(queue) < 1L) return(NULL)             liteq::try_consume(queue)         },          id_fn = function(msg) {             msg$id         },          data_fn = function(msg) {             jsonlite::fromJSON(msg$message)         },          commit_fn = function(msg, data) {             liteq::ack(msg)         },          abort_fn = function(msg, error = NULL, data = NULL) {             liteq::nack(msg)         },          done_fn = if (run_forever) {             function() FALSE         } else {             function() liteq::is_empty(queue)         }     ) }

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: Next, run background daemon continuously pulls jobs queue processes . example uses run_forever = TRUE keep polling indefinitely, .exit() ensure clean shutdown, pump_drain() process items without growing memory time. NOTE Stopping daemon. loop infinite, stop interrupt (Ctrl-C / SIGINT); .exit() hook shuts mirai workers cleanly. programmatic shutdown, publish sentinel “poison-pill” message whose handler flips flag done_fn checks.
code: run_mirai_daemon <- function(db_path,                               queue_name = "jobs",                               n_workers = 4,                              sleep_ms = 100) {     q <- liteq::ensure_queue(queue_name, db = db_path)      save_result <- function(task_id, result) {         # Persist to your database here         message("Task ", task_id, " -> ", result)     }      mirai::daemons(n_workers)     on.exit(mirai::daemons(0), add = TRUE)   # always release workers      pipeline <- make_liteq_source(q) |>         pump(           function(payload) {             payload$x * 10           },           backend = "mirai",            max_workers = n_workers,            buffer_size = n_workers         )      message("Daemon started. Polling queue: ", queue_name)     pump_drain(         pipeline,         handle_fn = function(id, data, ok) {             if (ok && !inherits(data, "error")) {                 save_result(id, data)             }         },         sleep_ms = sleep_ms   # backoff when the queue is empty     ) }

what: A note on liteq and parallel backends
title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: n_ready() guard added pull_fn() essential combining liteq parallel backend (mirai_backend(), future_backend(), parallel_backend()). liteq::try_consume() runs crash-recovery sweep whenever called READY message available. sweep probes lock file every -flight (WORKING) message blocks SQLite busy timeout (roughly 10 seconds) lock still held live consumer. parallel siphon stage deliberately keeps several messages WORKING jobs run workers, calling try_consume() window stalls daemon 10 seconds per -flight message - looks like hang. Guarding call consumes READY message actually exists avoids crash-recovery path entirely: NOTE use liteq::is_empty() guard: counts messages, including WORKING ones, stays FALSE jobs flight. Use done_fn (daemon finished messages remain ).
code: n_ready <- function(queue) {     msgs <- liteq::list_messages(queue)     if (nrow(msgs) == 0L) 0L else sum(msgs$status == "READY") }  pull_fn <- function() {     if (n_ready(queue) < 1L) return(NULL)     liteq::try_consume(queue) }

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: long-lived daemon, crashed worker process take whole daemon . parallel_backend() provides box: owns PSOCK cluster, worker dies replaces node, replays setup expressions registered parallel_setup_workers(), resubmits -flight job retries times. fits queue architecture naturally - liteq already gives -least-delivery via ack/nack, backend’s retry semantics also -least-, task handlers idempotent either way. use , swap backend inside daemon function: See ?parallel_backend full fault-tolerance contract.
code: bk <- parallel_backend(n_workers, retries = 3) parallel_setup_workers(bk, library(jsonlite)) on.exit(parallel_stop(bk), add = TRUE)  pipeline <- make_liteq_source(q) |>     pump(       function(payload) payload$x * 10,       backend = bk,       max_workers = n_workers,       buffer_size = n_workers     )

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: send work daemon, publish messages queue web application R process. Use liteq::publish() title (descriptive label) message (JSON payload): daemon consume messages, process parallel, call save_result() transformed data. title field useful logging debugging, message field contains actual task data.
code: library(liteq) library(jsonlite)  # Create or access the queue q <- liteq::ensure_queue("jobs", db = db_path)  # Publish a job liteq::publish(   q,   title = "Job 1",   message = jsonlite::toJSON(list(x = 5), auto_unbox = TRUE) )  # Publish multiple jobs liteq::publish(   q,   title = "Job 2",   message = jsonlite::toJSON(list(x = 10), auto_unbox = TRUE) ) liteq::publish(   q,   title = "Job 3",   message = jsonlite::toJSON(list(x = 15), auto_unbox = TRUE) )

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: Finally, need manual control, use registry directly. pump_managed_source() wrapper built top pump_item_registry(), stores source-owned objects item ID. approach pump_managed_source() uses internally:
code: make_liteq_source <- function(queue, run_forever = TRUE) {     registry <- pump_item_registry()      pump_source(         pull_fn = function() {             if (n_ready(queue) < 1L) return(NULL)             msg <- liteq::try_consume(queue)             if (is.null(msg)) return(NULL)              registry$set(msg$id, msg)              payload <- tryCatch(                 jsonlite::fromJSON(msg$message),                 error = function(e) e             )              list(                 id = msg$id,                 data = payload,                 ok = !inherits(payload, "error")             )         },          item_commit_fn = function(id, data) {             liteq::ack(registry$get(id))         },          item_abort_fn = function(id, error = NULL, data = NULL) {             liteq::nack(registry$get(id))         },          item_release_fn = function(id) {             registry$remove(id)         },          done_fn = if (run_forever) {             function() FALSE         } else {             function() liteq::is_empty(queue)         },          close_fn = function() {             registry$drain(function(id, msg) {                 try(liteq::nack(msg), silent = TRUE)             })         }     ) }

title: Asynchronous Daemons
text: architecture keeps web tier responsive background workers handle long-running tasks. bounds resources controlled concurrency backpressure, provides reliability via commit/abort semantics persistent queue, scales independently tuning workers-regardless queue parallel backend use. properties realize separation concerns introduced start.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: siphon runtime pull-based staged pipelines R. replacement future, mirai, parallel, execution backends. tools execute individual jobs; siphon connects stages, limits active jobs bounded slots, lets items move downstream stages soon ready. central use case pipeline different stages need different resources: CPU-heavy preparation many workers. GPU dispatcher must run main R thread. Parallel /O modest number workers.
code: library(siphon)  # Define common test items items <- 1:4

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: run simple pipeline, start source (1:10 list) add processing stage using pump(). Finally, execute pipeline using pump_run().
code: # Create and run the pipeline res <- items |>   pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  print(res) #. [[1]] #. [1] 2 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 4 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 6 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 8

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: Notice pump_run() returns results list matching exact order original input items, even execution asynchronous completed order.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: canonical multi-stage example maps directly scenario introduction. stage uses resource needs: prepare_cpu runs CPU-heavy work across several mirai workers, run_gpu dispatches main R thread single slot, write_output performs parallel /O mirai workers. pipeline pull-based, GPU stage can start first prepared item soon ready, even CPU jobs upstream stage finish. diagram shows runtime model: stage owns bounded number active slots, completed items move bounded output buffer, downstream stages pull work soon capacity available. staged siphon pipeline per-stage slots bounded buffers. define helper functions stages: using mirai parallel backends, can set pipeline follows:
code: # Define referenced helper functions prepare_cpu <- function(x) x + 10 run_gpu <- function(x) x * 5 write_output <- function(x) invisible(x) # Start mirai daemons for parallel execution mirai::daemons(2)  # Run the three-stage pipeline res <- items |>   pump(prepare_cpu, backend = mirai_backend(), max_workers = 2) |>   pump(run_gpu, backend = main_backend(), max_workers = 1) |>   pump(write_output, backend = mirai_backend(), max_workers = 2) |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  print(res) #. [[1]] #. [1] 55 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 60 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 65 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 70  # Shut down daemons mirai::daemons(0)

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: max_workers parameter specifies maximum number active processing slots (concurrent jobs) allowed stage one time. Resource management: can restrict concurrency resource-constrained operations (e.g., using synchronous main_backend() setting max_workers = 1 async backend GPU dispatch stage prevent duplicating contexts competing VRAM) letting stages run higher concurrency. Backend capacity: default, max_workers specified, defaults worker count associated backend. synchronous main_backend(), max_workers ignored always forced 1 since execution runs sequentially main R process. Note main thread coordination: using main_backend() stages like GPU dispatch, main thread executes jobs synchronously (one time), halt pipeline coordination. main thread continues poll coordinate stages main_backend() job executions, parallel stages async backends (e.g., mirai_backend()) continue processing concurrently. main_backend() stage sequential, overall pipeline maintains parallelism across stages.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: siphon supports multiple execution backends run jobs parallel different threads. Backends can specified either using constructor function (recommended) string alias: styles valid, string aliases automatically resolved internally. instance, following two specifications equivalent:
code: # Using constructor function f1 <- items |> pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = main_backend())  # Using string alias f2 <- items |> pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main")

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: can set pipeline-wide default backend via pump_run() pump_drain(). stage created pump(..., backend = NULL) (default), inherits backend set pump_run(..., backend = ...). explicit stage-level backend always overrides pipeline-wide default. useful want run entire pipeline specific backend without repeating backend specification stage: can still override default specific stages:
code: # Set a pipeline-wide default backend res <- items |>   pump(function(x) x * 2) |>  # Inherits "main" from pump_run   pump(function(x) x + 10) |> # Inherits "main" from pump_run   pump_run(backend = "main", verbose = FALSE)  print(res) #. [[1]] #. [1] 12 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 14 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 16 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 18 # Override the default for a specific stage res <- items |>   pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") |> # Explicit override   pump(function(x) x + 10) |>                  # Inherits "main" from pump_run   pump_run(backend = "main", verbose = FALSE)  print(res) #. [[1]] #. [1] 12 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 14 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 16 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 18

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: using async backends like mirai, future, parallel, responsible managing worker lifecycle. Failing shut workers can leave zombie processes open connections.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: mirai_backend(), start daemons mirai::daemons(n) shut mirai::daemons(0). Use .exit() automatic cleanup:
code: if (requireNamespace("mirai", quietly = TRUE)) {   mirai::daemons(2)   on.exit(mirai::daemons(0), add = TRUE)    res <- items |>     pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = mirai_backend()) |>     pump_run(verbose = FALSE) }

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: future_backend(), set plan future::plan() restore previous plan done:
code: if (requireNamespace("future", quietly = TRUE)) {   old_plan <- future::plan("sequential")   on.exit(future::plan(old_plan), add = TRUE)    res <- items |>     pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = future_backend()) |>     pump_run(verbose = FALSE) }

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: Unlike backends, parallel_backend() owns PSOCK cluster. worker process dies mid-job, backend replaces node fresh one, replays setup expressions registered parallel_setup_workers(), resubmits job, retries times. failed jobs resubmitted, execution follows -least-semantics: job functions side effects idempotent. cluster created backend constructed must released parallel_stop(): See ?parallel_backend full fault-tolerance contract, including handled (hung workers failures main R process).
code: bk <- parallel_backend(2, retries = 3) parallel_setup_workers(bk, offset <- 100)  res <- items |>   pump(function(x) x + offset, backend = bk) |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  print(res) #. [[1]] #. [1] 101 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 102 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 103 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 104 parallel_stop(bk)

what: Pooling strategies: one shared pool or isolated pools
title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: Among backends, parallel_backend() one whose worker pool object create (mirai daemons future plans process-global). pool topology pipeline therefore design choice, two patterns. One shared pool. Passing backend object several stages shares cluster — reusing one backend construction shared. max_workers sharing stages statically partition pool must sum worker count; since stage defaults full count, set explicitly every sharing stage. economical choice stages complementary duty cycles: pool n + 1 workers serving heavy read stage (max_workers = n) near-idle write stage (max_workers = 1) avoids paying whole extra cluster stage mostly waiting. Jobs shared pool node affinity — free node can take job sharing stage: two practical consequences. Worker state registered parallel_setup_workers() must cover needs every sharing stage, node may run stage’s jobs. node’s memory budget accommodate largest job sharing stage. Isolated pools. Creating separate backend per stage isolates pools: stage’s jobs run cluster nodes, deterministically. Choose isolation stages need different worker configurations: different hosts — parallel_backend(c("nodeA", "nodeB")) one stage, local workers another; different fault-tolerance settings (retries, retry_sleep); different worker state — heavy packages large objects loaded pool needs ; separate per-node memory budgets — node just held large intermediate result never takes another stage’s job. cost R processes one lifecycle manage.
code: bk <- parallel_backend(2)  res <- 1:6 |>   pump(function(x) {     Sys.sleep(0.02)     c(stage1_pid = Sys.getpid(), x = x)   }, backend = bk, max_workers = 1) |>   pump(function(v) {     Sys.sleep(0.02)     c(v, stage2_pid = Sys.getpid())   }, backend = bk, max_workers = 1) |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  stage1 <- unique(vapply(res, function(v) v[["stage1_pid"]], numeric(1))) stage2 <- unique(vapply(res, function(v) v[["stage2_pid"]], numeric(1)))  # often non-empty: nodes are pooled, not bound to a stage intersect(stage1, stage2) #. [1] 7437 parallel_stop(bk) bk_read <- parallel_backend(1) bk_write <- parallel_backend(1)  res <- 1:6 |>   pump(function(x) c(stage1_pid = Sys.getpid(), x = x),        backend = bk_read) |>   pump(function(v) c(v, stage2_pid = Sys.getpid()),        backend = bk_write) |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  stage1 <- unique(vapply(res, function(v) v[["stage1_pid"]], numeric(1))) stage2 <- unique(vapply(res, function(v) v[["stage2_pid"]], numeric(1)))  # always empty: pools are disjoint by construction intersect(stage1, stage2) #. numeric(0) parallel_stop(bk_read) parallel_stop(bk_write)

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: default, output buffer stage capped capacity (1000 items input size, whichever smaller). prevents excessive memory usage large infinite pipelines still allowing efficient throughput small ones. downstream stages slow, items large consume lot memory, can limit memory usage enable backpressure setting smaller buffer_size. output buffer stage full (.e. reached buffer_size), stage stops draining completed jobs. active workers remain busy, stage stops pulling new items upstream, propagating pressure backward pipeline. example makes backpressure visible. fast producer (Stage 1, two workers) feeds slow consumer (Stage 2, one worker), Stage 1’s output buffer capped buffer_size = 2. use pump_drain() (covered next section) stream items capture snapshot mid-run, moment third item reaches sink: source holds 20 items Stage 1 process almost instantly, yet output buffer pinned capacity (buffer: 2/2) source position far 20. full buffer stops Stage 1 draining completed jobs, turn stops pulling new items source - pressure propagates backward. Without buffer_size = 2 cap, Stage 1 race ahead hold 20 results memory . NOTE live snapshot concurrent pipeline, exact numbers vary run run. pattern - full upstream buffer source raced ahead - demonstrates backpressure.
code: mirai::daemons(2)  f <- 1:20 |>   pump(function(x) x, backend = mirai_backend(), max_workers = 2, buffer_size = 2) |>   pump(function(x) { Sys.sleep(0.03); x }, backend = mirai_backend(), max_workers = 1)  # Snapshot the live pipeline when the 3rd item is delivered snapshot <- NULL delivered <- 0 pump_drain(f, verbose = FALSE, handle_fn = function(id, data, ok) {   delivered <<- delivered + 1   if (delivered == 3) snapshot <<- pump_status(f) })  print(snapshot) #. <pump_status> #.   Source (main): #.     length:   20 #.     position: 7 #.     errors:   0 #.   Stage 1 (mirai): #.     workers: 2/2 #.     buffer:  1/2 #.     done:    5 #.     errors:  0 #.   Stage 2 (mirai): #.     workers: 1/1 #.     buffer:  0/20 #.     done:    3 #.     errors:  0 #.     polls:   3 hits, 12 misses (20% hit) #.     time:    93ms (fn: 90.6ms, idle: 2.4ms) #.  #.   Summary: #.     poll_wall_time: 5ms #.     fn:             91.1ms #.     idle:           2.9ms  mirai::daemons(0)

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: long-running infinite pipelines, pump_drain() provides memory-safe alternative pump_run(). Instead accumulating results list, passes item callback function soon ready:

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: long-running pipelines, pump_run() pump_drain() support progress reporting. verbose = TRUE (default) pipeline size large enough relative backend worker count, text progress bar printed console.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: Error handling controlled on_error, can set per stage pipeline-wide default via pump_run(): "collect": propagate error objects appear final result list. "stop": halt pipeline throw first error (default). "continue": drop failed items. stage created pump(..., on_error = NULL) (default), inherits policy set pump_run(..., on_error = ...). explicit stage-level on_error always overrides pipeline-wide default. fragile function throws error odd numbers:
code: fragile_fn <- function(x) {   if (x %% 2 != 0) {     stop("odd number error")   }   x }

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: stage sets on_error, pipeline stops first error:
code: items |>   pump(fragile_fn, backend = "main") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE) #. Error: #. ! odd number error

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: "collect", error objects preserved returned result list:
code: res_collect <- items |>   pump(fragile_fn, backend = "main") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE, on_error = "collect")  # The list contains both valid results and error objects print(res_collect) #. [[1]] #. <simpleError in (function (x) {    if (x%%2 != 0) {        stop("odd number error")    }    x})(1L): odd number error> #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 2 #.  #. [[3]] #. <simpleError in (function (x) {    if (x%%2 != 0) {        stop("odd number error")    }    x})(3L): odd number error> #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 4

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: "continue", failed items dropped entirely; result list shorter input preserves relative order successful items:
code: res_continue <- items |>   pump(fragile_fn, backend = "main") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE, on_error = "continue")  # Failed items are dropped print(res_continue) #. [[1]] #. [1] 2 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 4

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: stage can override pipeline-wide default. example, pipeline default "stop", Stage 1 explicitly collects error Stage 2 can process : NOTE pump_run(on_error = ...) pipeline-wide default. perform terminal error handling beyond stages already ; supplies default policy stages explicitly set on_error.
code: items |>   pump(fragile_fn, backend = "main", on_error = "collect") |>   pump(function(x) x + 10, backend = "main", on_error = "stop") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE) #. Error: #. ! odd number error

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: pump_status() returns snapshot pipeline’s internal state. Use monitor long-running pipelines diagnose time spent. report covers three things: source, stage upstream chain, global summary. Source length - total items source. position - many pulled far. errors - items seen ok = FALSE. stage workers_active / workers_limit - running jobs vs. stage’s slot limit. buffer_size / buffer_capacity - items output buffer vs. cap. completed - items finished stage. errors - items seen ok = FALSE. fn_time / idle_time - time (ms) running function vs. waiting. Summary poll_wall_time - total time (ms) spent coordinating inside next_item(). fn / idle - aggregate work wait time across stages.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: Call pump_status() run see pipeline behaved: glance, report confirms every item pulled (position), stage completed work errors, runtime split useful work (fn) waiting (idle). per-item work substantial, fn dominates; work trivial, idle (coordination) dominates - exactly siphon targets pipelines real work per item (see note ). pump_status() reads live state, can also call execution - example pump_drain() callback - monitor progress without stopping pipeline. NOTE Timing metrics exclude backoff sleep (sleep_ms argument pump_run() pump_drain()); total wall-clock time roughly poll_wall_time + poll_misses * sleep_ms. parallel backends, fn_time aggregates work across workers can exceed poll_wall_time, since workers run simultaneously main thread coordinates. siphon designed pipelines substantial work per item, coordination overhead negligible compared actual work time.
code: mirai::daemons(2)  f <- 1:5 |>   pump(     function(x) {        Sys.sleep(0.01)       x + 10     },      backend = "mirai",      max_workers = 2   ) |>   pump(     function(x) x * 2,      backend = "mirai",      max_workers = 2   )  pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE) #. [[1]] #. [1] 22 #.  #. [[2]] #. [1] 24 #.  #. [[3]] #. [1] 26 #.  #. [[4]] #. [1] 28 #.  #. [[5]] #. [1] 30  # Source position, per-stage completed/errors, and a fn-vs-idle timing summary print(pump_status(f)) #. <pump_status> #.   Source (main): #.     length:   5 #.     position: 5 #.     errors:   0 #.   Stage 1 (mirai): #.     workers: 0/2 #.     buffer:  0/5 #.     done:    5 #.     errors:  0 #.   Stage 2 (mirai): #.     workers: 0/2 #.     buffer:  0/5 #.     done:    5 #.     errors:  0 #.     polls:   5 hits, 6 misses (45.5% hit) #.     time:    2.7ms (fn: 0.1ms, idle: 2.6ms) #.  #.   Summary: #.     poll_wall_time: 4.5ms #.     fn:             51.1ms #.     idle:           3.6ms  mirai::daemons(0)

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: timeout parameter pump_run() checked cooperatively polling iterations main R thread. means: works async/parallel backends: timeout trigger control returned main loop (e.g. using mirai_backend(), future_backend() parallel plan, parallel_backend()). work synchronous backends: using main_backend() (sequential future plan), job stuck infinite loop blocking operation runs main thread, freezing execution preventing timeout ever checked. work C/C++ blocks: task blocks synchronously compiled C/C++ code within worker, timeout interrupt immediately. timeout suitable detecting stuck jobs running asynchronously, provide hard real-time guarantees support synchronous execution pipelines.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines with siphon
text: advanced use cases, can create custom sources connect external data systems (files, databases, message queues) using pump_source(). custom source requires pull_fn function returns list(id, data, ok) NULL (id must non-missing, scalar atomic value, uniqueness identifier user’s responsibility). complete example implementing database-backed queue source using liteq long-running daemon processes, see Asynchronous Daemons vignette (vignette("daemons", package = "siphon")).

code: @Manual{,   title = {siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines},   author = {Rolf Simoes},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.5.0},   url = {https://github.com/rolfsimoes/siphon}, }

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines
text: siphon small runtime pull-based staged pipelines R. replacement future, mirai, parallel, execution backends. tools execute jobs. siphon connects stages, limits active jobs slots, lets items move downstream stages soon ready.

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines
text: Install development version GitHub: published CRAN, install :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("rolfsimoes/siphon") install.packages("siphon") library(siphon)  # Define test data items <- 1:5  # Two-stage pipeline showing flow composition res <- items |>   pump(function(x) x + 1, backend = "main") |>   pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") |>   pump_run(verbose = FALSE)  # Results in original input order print(res) ## [[1]] ## [1] 4 ##  ## [[2]] ## [1] 6 ##  ## [[3]] ## [1] 8 ##  ## [[4]] ## [1] 10 ##  ## [[5]] ## [1] 12

title: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines
text: Pull-based staged pipelines - Items move downstream stages soon ready, without waiting upstream completion Multiple execution backends - Support main thread, mirai, future, parallel (PSOCK) backends Fault-tolerant execution - parallel_backend() replaces crashed workers resubmits jobs (-least-semantics) Backpressure control - Bounded buffers buffer_size limit memory usage Concurrency control - Per-stage max_workers manage resource constraints Error handling - Flexible error policies: "collect", "stop", "continue" Timeout protection - Optional timeout pump_run() avoid infinite polling Status inspection - pump_status() monitoring pipeline state Order preservation - Results returned original input order regardless execution order Custom sources - pump_source() connect external data sources (queues, databases, files) Streaming drain - pump_drain() memory-safe daemon-style processing Use direct parallel map tools problem simply lapply(x, f) workers. Use siphon problem staged flow different resource constraints per stage, CPU preparation, main-thread GPU dispatch, parallel /O. Note stages using main_backend() run sequentially main thread block pipeline coordination-stages async backends continue processing parallel. See vignette("siphon") longer introduction error handling status inspection examples.

title: Create a future backend — future_backend
text: future_backend() submits jobs future::future(). number available slots read future::nbrOfWorkers().

title: Create a future backend — future_backend
code: future_backend()

title: Create a future backend — future_backend
text: backend object.

title: Create a future backend — future_backend
text: Note: using future_backend(), responsible managing future plan lifecycle. Call future::plan() set plan restore previous plan done. See vignette examples. Fault tolerance delegated future framework: backend performs retries. worker dies running job, resulting FutureError surfaced pump_error value item (subject on_error policy) rather aborting pipeline. siphon-managed recovery retries, see parallel_backend().

title: Create a future backend — future_backend
code: if (requireNamespace("future", quietly = TRUE)) {     old_plan <- future::plan("sequential")     on.exit(future::plan(old_plan), add = TRUE)     f <- 1:5 |>         pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = future_backend())     pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE) } #> [[1]] #> [1] 2 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 4 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] 6 #>  #> [[4]] #> [1] 8 #>  #> [[5]] #> [1] 10 #>

title: Create a mirai backend — mirai_backend

title: Create a mirai backend — mirai_backend
text: Note: using mirai_backend(), responsible managing mirai daemon lifecycle. Call mirai::daemons(n) start workers mirai::daemons(0) shut . See vignette examples. Fault tolerance delegated mirai framework: backend performs retries. daemon dies running job, resulting errorValue surfaced pump_error value item (subject on_error policy) rather leaking pipeline. siphon-managed recovery retries, see parallel_backend().

title: Create a mirai backend — mirai_backend
code: if (requireNamespace("mirai", quietly = TRUE) &&     mirai::status()$connections > 0) {     f <- 1:5 |>         pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = mirai_backend())     pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE) }

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
text: parallel_backend() creates manages PSOCK cluster submits jobs . Jobs dispatched non-blocking send readiness polled socketSelect(), main process never blocks jobs run cluster nodes.

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
code: parallel_backend(workers, ..., retries = 3L, retry_sleep = 0)

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
text: workers number local worker processes start, character vector host names run workers (accepted parallel::makePSOCKcluster()). ... Additional arguments passed parallel::makePSOCKcluster(). retries Number times job resubmitted worker connection failure job marked failed. non-negative integer. retry_sleep Seconds wait retry attempt. non-negative number.

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
text: backend object.

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
text: backend owns cluster: created parallel_backend() called must shut parallel_stop() longer needed. node runs one job time, max_workers stage using backend must exceed number workers (enforced pump()). Passing backend several pump() stages shares cluster — normal way run multi-stage pipeline one worker pool. Node availability tracked backend level results received per node, concurrent stages cross-wire. one sizing rule max_workers stages sharing backend must sum number workers; since stage's max_workers defaults full worker count, set explicitly every sharing stage (e.g. pool n + 1 workers serving read stage max_workers = n write stage max_workers = 1). Oversubscribing fails dispatch time free-node invariant error. Jobs shared pool node affinity; isolate worker pools per stage (different hosts, retry settings, worker state), create separate backend stage instead — see "Pooling strategies" section vignette("siphon"). backend uses unexported sendCall() recvResult() functions parallel package communicate cluster nodes.

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
text: backend fault tolerant worker process failures: worker connection fails job running job dispatched (e.g. node died idle), backend replaces node fresh one resubmits job, retries times, sleeping retry_sleep seconds attempt. Setup expressions registered parallel_setup_workers() replayed replacement nodes. retries exhausted, job failed running yields error value (pump_error condition) instead failing pipeline, node restored subsequent jobs; job dispatched raises error. failed jobs resubmitted, execution follows -least-semantics: job's side effects (file writes, database inserts, API calls) may run worker dies effect result delivered. Job functions idempotent. following failures handled: hung workers (failure detection connection-based, worker stalls without dying never detected job timeout) failures main R process (persistence checkpointing; -flight work lost).

title: Create a parallel (PSOCK) backend — parallel_backend
code: if (requireNamespace("parallel", quietly = TRUE)) {     bk <- parallel_backend(2)     f <- 1:5 |>         pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = bk)     pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE)     parallel_stop(bk)      # one pool shared by two stages (max_workers sum == worker count)     bk <- parallel_backend(2)     f <- 1:5 |>         pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = bk, max_workers = 1) |>         pump(function(x) x + 1, backend = bk, max_workers = 1)     pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE)     parallel_stop(bk) }

what: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
text: parallel_eval_workers() evaluates expression global environment every worker parallel backend returns per-worker results, like parallel::clusterEvalQ(). Unlike clusterEvalQ(), values calling frame can injected wrapping double braces, e.g. parallel_eval_workers(bk, x + {{ y }}) evaluates x + <value y> worker.

title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
code: parallel_eval_workers(backend, expr)

title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
text: backend backend object created parallel_backend(). expr expression evaluate worker.

title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
text: list one element per worker, worker order.

title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
text: expression captured unevaluated broadcast: dispatched every worker result collected, total wall time bounded slowest worker rather sum workers. Unlike parallel_setup_workers(), expression recorded replay replacement nodes created worker failure. Use parallel_setup_workers() state jobs depend (packages, options, objects) survives worker recovery; use parallel_eval_workers() one-shot queries (diagnostics, versions, process ids) warm-work whose loss replaced node acceptable. Evaluation can run jobs active backend. expression fails worker, error raised naming failing workers.

title: Evaluate an expression on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_eval_workers
code: if (requireNamespace("parallel", quietly = TRUE)) {     bk <- parallel_backend(2)     offset <- 40     parallel_eval_workers(bk, 2 + {{ offset }})     parallel_stop(bk) }

what: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
text: parallel_setup_workers() evaluates expression global environment every worker parallel backend. Use load packages, source files, define objects jobs need. expression recorded replayed automatically replacement node created worker failure.

title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
code: parallel_setup_workers(backend, expr)

title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
text: backend backend object created parallel_backend(). expr expression evaluate worker.

title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
text: backend, invisibly.

title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
text: expression captured unevaluated. Values calling frame can injected wrapping double braces, e.g. parallel_setup_workers(bk, x <- {{ y }}) assigns current value y x worker. Setup can run jobs active backend.

title: Run setup code on all workers of a parallel backend — parallel_setup_workers
code: if (requireNamespace("parallel", quietly = TRUE)) {     bk <- parallel_backend(2)     parallel_setup_workers(bk, threshold <- 10)     f <- 1:5 |>         pump(function(x) x + threshold, backend = bk)     pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE)     parallel_stop(bk) }

title: Stop a parallel backend — parallel_stop

title: Stop a parallel backend — parallel_stop
code: if (requireNamespace("parallel", quietly = TRUE)) {     bk <- parallel_backend(2)     parallel_stop(bk) }

what: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
title: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
text: pump() creates pull-based stage. stage pulls items upstream source free slots, submits jobs selected backend, yields completed items downstream stages.

title: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
code: pump(   x,   fn,   ...,   backend = NULL,   max_workers = NULL,   on_error = NULL,   buffer_size = NULL )

title: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
text: x pump object finite R object (list, vector) implicitly wrapped basic source. fn function. receives one item first argument. ... Additional arguments passed fn. backend backend object one "main", "mirai", "future". Use parallel_backend() directly fault-tolerant PSOCK execution (string alias). NULL (default), stage inherits backend set pump_run() pump_drain(). max_workers Maximum number active jobs stage. Defaults backend worker count. Ignored synchronous main_backend() (always uses 1). on_error handle item errors: "stop" throws first error, "collect" propagates , "continue" drops failed items. NULL (default), stage inherits policy set pump_run(). buffer_size Maximum size output buffer. Defaults min(length(x), 1000L). Use smaller value enable true backpressure.

title: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
text: pump object.

title: Add a processing stage to a pipeline — pump
code: # Single stage pipeline f <- 1:5 |> pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE) #> [[1]] #> [1] 2 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 4 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] 6 #>  #> [[4]] #> [1] 8 #>  #> [[5]] #> [1] 10 #>   # Two-stage pipeline f <- 1:5 |>     pump(function(x) x + 1, backend = "main") |>     pump(function(x) x * 3, backend = "main") pump_run(f, verbose = FALSE) #> [[1]] #> [1] 6 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 9 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] 12 #>  #> [[4]] #> [1] 15 #>  #> [[5]] #> [1] 18 #>

title: Drain a siphon pipeline — pump_drain
text: pump_drain() runs pipeline, pulling items passing callback function become ready. memory-safe alternative pump_run() suitable infinite long-running pipelines.

title: Drain a siphon pipeline — pump_drain
code: pump_drain(x, handle_fn, sleep_ms = 10, verbose = TRUE, backend = "main")

title: Drain a siphon pipeline — pump_drain
text: x pump object finite R object. handle_fn callback function signature function(id, data, ok) called completed item. sleep_ms Delay milliseconds polls item ready. verbose TRUE, show text progress bar. backend Default backend stages explicitly set backend. Can backend object one "main", "mirai", "future". Use parallel_backend() directly fault-tolerant PSOCK execution (string alias). Defaults "main".

title: Drain a siphon pipeline — pump_drain
text: Invisible NULL.

title: Create a managed custom source — pump_managed_source
text: pull_fn Function returns one source-owned item NULL. id_fn Function extracts item id. data_fn Function extracts serializable data pipeline. commit_fn Function called item accepted terminal runner. abort_fn Function called item leaves runtime acceptance. release_fn Optional function called item-level tracking released. done_fn Optional source-level done function. close_fn Optional source-level close function. length Source length function returning source length.

title: Create a managed custom source — pump_managed_source

what: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
text: pump_run() drains siphon pipeline upstream sources, active slots, output buffers complete. Results collected original input order using internal sequential indices (idx).

title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
code: pump_run(   x,   sleep_ms = 10,   verbose = TRUE,   on_error = "stop",   backend = "main",   timeout = NULL )

title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
text: x pump object finite R object. sleep_ms Delay polls item ready. verbose TRUE, show text progress bar. on_error Default error handling policy stages explicitly set on_error: "stop" throws first error, "collect" propagates error objects, "continue" drops failed items. Defaults "stop". backend Default backend stages explicitly set backend. Can backend object one "main", "mirai", "future". Use parallel_backend() directly fault-tolerant PSOCK execution (string alias). Defaults "main". timeout Maximum time seconds wait completion. NULL (default), waits indefinitely. exceeded, throws error.

title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
text: list results input order. Items dropped "continue" stage omitted entirely; result may shorter input.

title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run
text: timeout parameter checked cooperatively polling iterations main R thread. , works using asynchronous backends (mirai_backend(), future_backend() parallel plan, parallel_backend()) return control main loop. using synchronous backend (like main_backend()), job stuck infinite loop blocking operation freeze thread prevent timeout checked. Furthermore, timeout interrupt blocking C/C++ code within background workers. sleep_ms parameter introduces backoff time polls item ready. necessary CPU efficiency adds overhead. fast operations (e.g., simple arithmetic), overhead can dominate total runtime. siphon designed pipelines substantial work per item (e.g., /O, complex computations, external API calls) coordination overhead negligible compared actual work time.

title: Run a siphon pipeline and collect results — pump_run

what: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
text: pump_source() creates custom pull-based source use pump() pipelines. Use connect external data sources message queues, databases, file readers siphon pipeline.

title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
code: pump_source(   pull_fn,   done_fn = NULL,   close_fn = NULL,   length = Inf,   item_commit_fn = NULL,   item_abort_fn = NULL,   item_release_fn = NULL )

title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
text: pull_fn function arguments returns list(id, data, ok) NULL. returned list must contain non-missing, scalar atomic user-visible id (uniqueness user's responsibility), data object, non-missing scalar logical ok flag indicating success. done_fn function arguments returning TRUE FALSE. Defaults NULL (source never finishes ). close_fn optional function arguments resource cleanup (e.g., closing file connections database handles). Called automatically pump_run() pump_drain() execution completes. length total number items expect. Defaults Inf. Used pump_run() result pre-allocation progress reporting. item_commit_fn optional function called item successfully completes pipeline. Receives id data arguments. Used acknowledging external resources (e.g., message queue ack). item_abort_fn optional function called item fails. Receives id, error, data arguments. Used rejecting external resources (e.g., message queue nack). item_release_fn optional function called item released pipeline (e.g., timeout shutdown). Receives id argument.

title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
text: pump object can piped pump().

title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
text: pull_fn function called repeatedly downstream stages retrieve items. return list(id, data, ok) item available, NULL item ready. source considered infinite default (suitable daemon-style processing); pass done_fn signal source exhausted.

title: Create a custom siphon source — pump_source
code: # A simple counter source counter_source <- function(n) {     i <- 0L     pump_source(         pull_fn = function() {             if (i >= n) {                 return(NULL)             }             i <<- i + 1L             list(id = i, data = i, ok = TRUE)         },         done_fn = function() i >= n,         length = n     ) } src <- counter_source(5) res <- src |>     pump(function(x) x * 2) |>     pump_run(verbose = FALSE) print(res) #> [[1]] #> [1] 2 #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] 4 #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] 6 #>  #> [[4]] #> [1] 8 #>  #> [[5]] #> [1] 10 #>

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
text: pump_status() returns snapshot pump object's internal state, including detailed timing metrics help identify time spent.

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
code: pump_status(x)

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
text: x pump object.

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
text: list : buffer_size - current items output buffer. buffer_capacity - maximum buffer size. workers_active - currently running jobs. workers_limit - maximum concurrent jobs stage. completed - items finished stage. errors - items ok = FALSE seen stage. poll_hits - number successful polls (items ready). poll_misses - number failed polls (item ready). poll_wall_time - total time spent inside next_item() calls milliseconds. fn_time - wall-clock time spent executing user function (useful work) milliseconds. idle_time - time spent waiting (starvation exhaustion) milliseconds.

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
text: Note poll_wall_time excludes time spent backoff sleeps (controlled sleep_ms parameter pump_run() pump_drain()). Total wall-clock time using pump_run() approximately poll_wall_time + poll_misses * sleep_ms. fast operations, backoff overhead can dominate total runtime. siphon designed pipelines substantial work per item coordination overhead negligible compared actual work time.

title: Pipeline status — pump_status
code: f <- 1:5 |> pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") pump_status(f) #> <pump_status> #>   Source (main): #>     length:   5 #>     position: 0 #>     errors:   0 #>   Stage 1 (main): #>     workers: 0/1 #>     buffer:  0/5 #>     done:    0 #>     errors:  0

title: siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines — siphon-package
text: siphon provides pull-based pipeline runtime items flow chain stages. stage pulls work upstream source free slots, submits jobs backend (main, mirai, future, parallel), yields completed items downstream. Results collected pump_run() original input order via sequential internal indices (idx).

title: siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines — siphon-package
text: small runtime pull-based staged pipelines. siphon connects item streams asynchronous stages bounded slots backend-specific execution.

title: siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines — siphon-package
text: main_backend() Executes jobs synchronously current R process (default) mirai_backend() Submits jobs mirai::mirai() async execution future_backend() Submits jobs future::future() async execution parallel_backend() Owns PSOCK cluster fault tolerance worker failures; use parallel_setup_workers() parallel_stop()

title: siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines — siphon-package
text: stage can configure error handling via on_error parameter, inherit pipeline-wide default pump_run(..., on_error = ...): "stop" Throws first error (default pump_run) "collect" Propagates error objects "continue" Drops failed items Explicit stage-level on_error always overrides pump_run() default.

title: siphon: Pull-Based Staged Pipelines — siphon-package
text: Maintainer: Rolf Simoes rolfsimoes@gmail.com Authors: Rolf Simoes rolfsimoes@gmail.com

title: siphon 0.5.0
text: Added parallel_eval_workers(), equivalent parallel::clusterEvalQ() parallel backends: evaluates expression global environment every worker returns per-worker results. Supports injecting values calling frame {{ }}, like parallel_setup_workers(). Unlike setup expressions, evaluations recorded replay replacement nodes. Corrected parallel_backend() documentation: one backend can shared across multiple pump() stages — results received per node cross-wire. requirement max_workers sharing stages sum worker count (stage defaults full count, set explicitly). Documented example covered tests, including failure mode pool oversubscribed. New vignette section “Pooling strategies: one shared pool isolated pools” documenting share one parallel_backend() across stages versus creating separate backend per stage isolate worker pools (job placement, worker state, sizing trade-offs). Worker setup evaluation now broadcast: expressions dispatched nodes results collected, parallel_setup_workers() parallel_eval_workers() complete time slowest worker instead sum workers. Per-node ordering setup expressions preserved, replay replacement nodes unchanged.

title: siphon 0.4.2
text: pump() adds processing stage pipeline. pump_run() executes pipeline collects results. pump_source() creates custom input sources using closures. pump_status() returns snapshot stage state. Added new pump_drain() function run pipelines without accumulating items memory, suitable persistent daemon processes. Implemented S3 generic method length.pump() (replaces $size() method) R-idiomatic dataset length queries.

title: siphon 0.4.2
text: Decoupled total pipeline length intermediate buffer_size prevent memory bloat coercion errors infinite pipelines. Stage buffer_size now defaults min(length(x), 1000L), capping memory usage large/infinite sources allocating correctly small ones. Custom sources created via pump_source() support resource cleanup callbacks via close_fn argument. Clean custom sources automatically upon pipeline exit pump_run() pump_drain(). Fixed progress bar pump_run() correctly suppress small inputs. Fixed empty input semantics; pump_run() now returns list() zero-length sources instead throwing error. Fixed per-stage on_error = "stop" ensure honored stage instead final stage. Added timeout parameter pump_run() avoid infinite polling stuck R jobs. Added requireNamespace() checks mirai, future, parallel backends clear error messages. Added async-backend scheduling tests mirai, future, parallel backends.
